news_summary: Innovative advancements in sustainable materials, recycling programs, and critical mineral management are driving progress in renewable energy and waste reduction.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées innovantes en matière de matériaux durables, de programmes de recyclage et de gestion des minéraux essentiels sont le moteur des progrès réalisés dans les domaines des énergies renouvelables et de la réduction des déchets.
news_summary_es: Los avances innovadores en materiales sostenibles, los programas de reciclaje y la gestión de minerales críticos están impulsando el progreso en el ámbito de las energías renovables y la reducción de residuos.

articles.title: Electronic-resonance enhanced molecule for perovskite solar cells
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 22 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10919-4Electronic-resonance enhanced molecule for perovskite solar cells
articles.content: Self-assembly monolayers (SAMs), which anchor to transparent conductive oxide (TCO) substrate and form an interfacial molecular dipole to extract carriers from perovskite layer, has promoted a stepwise improvement in efficiency of perovskite solar cells (pero-SCs).1-5 However, the limited intrinsic bonding strength due to constrained electron density on coordination sites incurs SAMs desorption and compromises charge extraction under operational stressors, posing a notable challenge to their long-term stability.6,7 To address this, we design a SAM with donor-acceptor-donor (D-A-D) resonant molecular structure, in which the electronic resonance increases the negative charge density at the acceptor anchoring group, significantly strengthening the phosphonic acid-indium tin oxide (ITO) anchoring bond and preventing the SAM desorption during operation. The device applying D-A-D resonant SAM possesses remarkable operational stability with negligible decay under maximum-power-point tracking (MPPT) at 85±5 °C for 1,080 h, maintains >93% after 1,080 h under metal halide (MH) lamp illumination (100 mW cm−2, 4.4% UV inside) at 85±5 °C and also retains >98% after 720 repetitive thermal cycles between −40 °C and 85 °C. Concurrently, the resonance induced charge delocalization facilitates efficient carrier transport, realizing a certified power conversion efficiencies (PCEs) of 27.69% on 0.063 cm2 devices and 23.63% with aperture area of 15.64 cm2. The certified efficiency of 26.64% is also realized on flexible substrates (0.063 cm2), demonstrating the universality of this approach across different types of substrates.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough in perovskite solar cells that enhances their efficiency and operational stability, which can lead to more reliable and efficient renewable energy solutions with broad environmental benefits.
articles.personality_title: New molecule design boosts efficiency and durability of perovskite solar cells
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Perovskite solar cells are a promising type of solar technology that can convert sunlight into electricity efficiently. A key part of these cells is a thin layer called a self-assembled monolayer (SAM), which helps move electric charges but can weaken over time, reducing the cell’s performance.

**What happened** – Scientists created a new SAM molecule with a special structure called donor-acceptor-donor (D-A-D). This design increases the electrical charge where the molecule attaches to the solar cell’s surface, making the bond much stronger. This prevents the molecule from falling off during use, even under heat and light stress. The new solar cells showed high stability, keeping over 93% of their power after 45 days of continuous testing at 85°C and strong light. They also maintained efficiency after many temperature changes and worked well on flexible materials.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it solves a common problem where solar cells lose power over time due to weak bonds in the SAM layer. By making the molecules stick better and carry charges more efficiently, the solar cells can last longer and produce more electricity. The best devices reached nearly 28% efficiency, which is very high for this type of solar cell.

**What's next step** – Researchers will likely explore using this new molecule design in larger and more practical solar panels. They may also test how these improved cells perform outdoors over longer periods. The approach could help make perovskite solar cells more reliable for everyday use.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new molecule design strengthens key layers in perovskite solar cells, boosting their efficiency and making them last longer under real-world conditions.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle molécule améliore l'efficacité et la durabilité des cellules solaires pérovskites
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les cellules solaires pérovskites sont une technologie prometteuse qui transforme efficacement la lumière du soleil en électricité. Une couche fine appelée monocouche auto-assemblée (SAM) aide à déplacer les charges électriques, mais elle peut s’affaiblir avec le temps, réduisant la performance de la cellule.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont créé une nouvelle molécule SAM avec une structure spéciale appelée donneur-accepteur-donneur (D-A-D). Cette conception augmente la charge électrique là où la molécule se fixe à la surface de la cellule, renforçant beaucoup le lien. Cela empêche la molécule de se détacher pendant l’utilisation, même sous la chaleur et la lumière. Les nouvelles cellules solaires ont montré une grande stabilité, conservant plus de 93 % de leur puissance après 45 jours de test continu à 85 °C et forte lumière. Elles ont aussi gardé leur efficacité après de nombreux changements de température et ont bien fonctionné sur des matériaux flexibles.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle résout un problème courant où les cellules solaires perdent de la puissance à cause de liens faibles dans la couche SAM. En renforçant l’adhérence des molécules et en facilitant le transport des charges, les cellules solaires durent plus longtemps et produisent plus d’électricité. Les meilleurs dispositifs ont atteint près de 28 % d’efficacité, ce qui est très élevé pour ce type de cellule.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement essayer d’utiliser cette nouvelle molécule dans des panneaux solaires plus grands et plus pratiques. Ils pourraient aussi tester ces cellules améliorées en extérieur sur de plus longues périodes. Cette approche pourrait rendre les cellules pérovskites plus fiables pour un usage quotidien.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Une nouvelle molécule renforce les couches clés des cellules solaires pérovskites, augmentant leur efficacité et leur durée de vie dans des conditions réelles.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo diseño molecular mejora la eficiencia y durabilidad de las células solares de perovskita
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las células solares de perovskita son una tecnología prometedora que convierte la luz solar en electricidad de manera eficiente. Una capa delgada llamada monocapa autoensamblada (SAM) ayuda a mover las cargas eléctricas, pero puede debilitarse con el tiempo, reduciendo el rendimiento de la célula.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos crearon una nueva molécula SAM con una estructura especial llamada donador-aceptor-donador (D-A-D). Este diseño aumenta la carga eléctrica donde la molécula se une a la superficie de la célula solar, haciendo el enlace mucho más fuerte. Esto evita que la molécula se desprenda durante el uso, incluso bajo calor y luz. Las nuevas células solares mostraron alta estabilidad, manteniendo más del 93 % de su potencia después de 45 días de pruebas continuas a 85 °C y luz intensa. También mantuvieron la eficiencia tras muchos cambios de temperatura y funcionaron bien en materiales flexibles.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento es importante porque resuelve un problema común donde las células solares pierden potencia con el tiempo debido a enlaces débiles en la capa SAM. Al hacer que las moléculas se adhieran mejor y transporten cargas más eficientemente, las células solares duran más y producen más electricidad. Los mejores dispositivos alcanzaron casi un 28 % de eficiencia, lo cual es muy alto para este tipo de célula.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente usarán este nuevo diseño molecular en paneles solares más grandes y prácticos. También podrían probar cómo funcionan estas células mejoradas al aire libre durante períodos más largos. Este enfoque podría ayudar a que las células solares de perovskita sean más confiables para el uso diario.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un nuevo diseño molecular fortalece capas clave en células solares de perovskita, aumentando su eficiencia y durabilidad bajo condiciones reales.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing, intricately woven molecular lattice hovering above a smooth, translucent surface symbolizing the transparent conductive oxide, with vibrant energy waves flowing seamlessly through interconnected donor-acceptor-donor structures, surrounded by soft, abstract representations of stable solar cells enduring gentle thermal cycles and bright, filtered light.

articles.title: Best of Sustainability In Your Ear: Colorado’s First-Of-Its-Kind EPR Oil Recycling Program With David Lawes
articles.summary:
Subscribe to receive transcripts by email. Read along with this episode. Americans dispose of approximately...
The post Best of Sustainability In Your Ear: Colorado’s First-Of-Its-Kind EPR Oil Recycling Program With David Lawes appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
Subscribe to receive transcripts by email. Read along with this episode.

Americans dispose of approximately 1.3 billion gallons of used motor oil annually, but only about 800 million gallons get recycled, and most of that is burned as fuel rather than re-refined into new oil. The plastic packaging oil comes in is more problematic: most curbside programs won’t accept them because residual oil contaminates other recyclables. What happens when the companies that make motor oil embrace extended producer responsibility (EPR) laws that require recycling the oil and the containers it comes in? David Lawes, CEO of the Lubricants Packaging Management Association (LPMA), is leading what could become a national model for extended producer responsibility. Colorado just became the testing ground. In September 2024, five major oil companies—BP Lubricants, Chevron, ExxonMobil, Shell, and Valvoline—founded LPMA as an independent producer responsibility organization.

Colorado gave producers a choice: join the Circular Action Alliance, which manages all packaging and printed paper recycling in the state, or develop their own sector-specific program that demonstrates better outcomes. LPMA chose the independent path, arguing that petroleum packaging requires specialized handling that general-purpose programs can’t provide efficiently. Lawes brings two decades of EPR policy experience to the role, including a decade regulating EPR programs in Canada. The program he ran in British Columbia achieves a 96% recycling rate for oil containers—compared to less than 1% in most U.S. states. “This is not about skirting the law or finding an easier pathway,” Lawes explains. “It is about meeting the same results in an industry-friendly way.”

If Colorado’s model works, it could reshape EPR programs nationwide. We discuss why petroleum packaging can’t be managed through curbside programs, what lessons from Canada’s more developed EPR system apply here, and whether the U.S. needs national recycling standards to harmonize the patchwork of state regulations. You can learn more about LPMA at interchange360.com.

Subscribe to Sustainability In Your Ear on iTunes

Follow Sustainability In Your Ear on Spreaker, iHeartRadio, or YouTube

Editor’s Note: This episode originally aired on December 1, 2025.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a pioneering extended producer responsibility (EPR) program in Colorado for recycling motor oil and its packaging, led by major oil companies. This initiative addresses a significant environmental issue by improving recycling rates and reducing contamination in curbside programs. The program has potential for broad impact if adopted nationally, demonstrating meaningful progress in sustainable waste management and circular economy practices.
articles.personality_title: Colorado launches first program to recycle motor oil and containers with oil companies' support
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Americans throw away about 1.3 billion gallons of used motor oil every year, but only some of it gets recycled. Most recycled oil is burned for fuel instead of being cleaned and reused. The plastic containers oil comes in are hard to recycle because leftover oil contaminates other recyclables, so most curbside recycling programs won’t take them.

**What happened** – In September 2024, five big oil companies—BP, Chevron, ExxonMobil, Shell, and Valvoline—started a group called the Lubricants Packaging Management Association (LPMA). They created a new program in Colorado to recycle both used motor oil and its containers. Colorado gave companies the choice to join a general recycling group or make a special program for oil packaging. LPMA chose to make their own because oil containers need special care. The program is led by David Lawes, who has 20 years of experience in extended producer responsibility (EPR), a system where companies take responsibility for recycling their products.

**Impact** – This program is special because it focuses on recycling oil packaging, which most programs don’t handle well. In Canada, similar programs recycle 96% of oil containers, while in the U.S. it’s less than 1%. If Colorado’s program works, it could become a model for other states to improve how motor oil and containers are recycled, reducing waste and pollution.

**What's next step** – The next step is to see how well the Colorado program works in practice. If it succeeds, other states might copy it. There is also a discussion about creating national recycling rules to make it easier for companies and states to manage oil recycling the same way across the country.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Colorado’s new program led by oil companies could change how America recycles motor oil and its containers, making the process cleaner and more effective.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le Colorado lance le premier programme de recyclage de l’huile moteur et de ses contenants avec le soutien des compagnies pétrolières
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque année, les Américains jettent environ 1,3 milliard de gallons d’huile moteur usagée, mais seule une partie est recyclée. La plupart de l’huile recyclée est brûlée pour produire du carburant au lieu d’être purifiée et réutilisée. Les contenants en plastique de l’huile sont difficiles à recycler car l’huile résiduelle contamine les autres matériaux recyclables, donc la plupart des programmes de recyclage en bordure de rue ne les acceptent pas.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – En septembre 2024, cinq grandes compagnies pétrolières—BP, Chevron, ExxonMobil, Shell et Valvoline—ont créé une association appelée Lubricants Packaging Management Association (LPMA). Ils ont lancé un nouveau programme au Colorado pour recycler à la fois l’huile moteur usagée et ses contenants. Le Colorado a donné aux entreprises le choix de rejoindre un groupe général de recyclage ou de créer un programme spécial pour les emballages d’huile. LPMA a choisi de créer son propre programme car ces contenants nécessitent un traitement spécial. Ce programme est dirigé par David Lawes, qui a 20 ans d’expérience en responsabilité élargie des producteurs (REP), un système où les entreprises sont responsables du recyclage de leurs produits.

**Impact** – Ce programme est unique car il se concentre sur le recyclage des emballages d’huile, ce que la plupart des programmes ne font pas bien. Au Canada, des programmes similaires recyclent 96 % des contenants d’huile, alors qu’aux États-Unis, c’est moins de 1 %. Si le programme du Colorado réussit, il pourrait servir de modèle pour d’autres États afin d’améliorer le recyclage de l’huile moteur et de ses contenants, réduisant ainsi les déchets et la pollution.

**Prochaine étape** – La prochaine étape est d’observer les résultats du programme au Colorado. S’il fonctionne bien, d’autres États pourraient l’adopter. Il y a aussi des discussions pour créer des règles nationales de recyclage afin de faciliter la gestion uniforme du recyclage de l’huile à travers le pays.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le nouveau programme du Colorado, dirigé par des compagnies pétrolières, pourrait transformer le recyclage de l’huile moteur et de ses contenants aux États-Unis en le rendant plus propre et efficace.
articles.personality_title_es: Colorado lanza el primer programa para reciclar aceite de motor y envases con apoyo de compañías petroleras
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los estadounidenses desechan alrededor de 1.3 mil millones de galones de aceite de motor usado cada año, pero solo una parte se recicla. La mayoría del aceite reciclado se quema para combustible en lugar de limpiarse y reutilizarse. Los envases plásticos del aceite son difíciles de reciclar porque el aceite residual contamina otros materiales reciclables, por lo que la mayoría de los programas de reciclaje en la acera no los aceptan.

**Qué pasó** – En septiembre de 2024, cinco grandes compañías petroleras—BP, Chevron, ExxonMobil, Shell y Valvoline—crearon un grupo llamado Lubricants Packaging Management Association (LPMA). Lanzaron un nuevo programa en Colorado para reciclar tanto el aceite de motor usado como sus envases. Colorado dio a las empresas la opción de unirse a un grupo general de reciclaje o crear un programa especial para envases de aceite. LPMA eligió hacer su propio programa porque los envases de petróleo necesitan un manejo especial. El programa está dirigido por David Lawes, quien tiene 20 años de experiencia en responsabilidad extendida del productor (EPR), un sistema donde las empresas se responsabilizan por reciclar sus productos.

**Impacto** – Este programa es especial porque se centra en reciclar los envases de aceite, algo que la mayoría de los programas no hacen bien. En Canadá, programas similares reciclan el 96% de los envases de aceite, mientras que en EE.UU. es menos del 1%. Si el programa de Colorado funciona, podría ser un modelo para otros estados y mejorar cómo se recicla el aceite de motor y sus envases, reduciendo residuos y contaminación.

**Próximo paso** – El siguiente paso es ver qué tan bien funciona el programa en Colorado. Si tiene éxito, otros estados podrían copiarlo. También se está discutiendo crear reglas nacionales de reciclaje para facilitar que las empresas y estados manejen el reciclaje de aceite de la misma manera en todo el país.

**Resumen en una frase** – El nuevo programa de Colorado, liderado por compañías petroleras, podría cambiar cómo se recicla el aceite de motor y sus envases en Estados Unidos, haciendo el proceso más limpio y efectivo.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of a circular flow of motor oil containers transforming into clean, renewed oil droplets, surrounded by abstract silhouettes of five interconnected oil company logos symbolized as five interlocking gears, set against a stylized Colorado mountain landscape with soft natural earth tones.

articles.title: The New Fix for Takeout Packaging Waste: Reusable-Container Networks
articles.summary:
The disposable containers, cups, lids, and utensils that U.S. restaurants hand out add up to...
The post The New Fix for Takeout Packaging Waste: Reusable-Container Networks appeared first on Earth911.
articles.content:
The disposable containers, cups, lids, and utensils that U.S. restaurants hand out add up to nearly 9 million tons of waste a year — about the weight of 25 Empire State Buildings — and close to four-fifths of that comes from takeout and delivery, according to the nonprofit Upstream’s Reuse Wins report.

A few years ago, two apps set out to shrink that pile by letting diners rate restaurants on their packaging. PlasticScore and Jybe each asked diners to score takeout packaging, ranking restaurants by how much single-use plastic and foam they handed out. The rating model leaned on a hard requirement: enough reviews in a given area to make the scores useful. In most cities that critical mass never arrived, and the apps stalled.

Today both have disappeared, but a different approach has taken hold: services that replace throwaway containers with reusable ones you borrow and return.

Borrow, Return, Repeat

The services gaining ground focus on durable containers built for repeated use. You order takeout in a reusable container, often a BPA-free polypropylene rated for around 1,000 uses, and return it to any participating location, and the operator washes it and puts it back into circulation.

The environmental payoff depends on how often each container is used. A University of Michigan life-cycle study, published in Resources, Conservation & Recycling in 2023, found that the environmental impact of reusable containers break even compared to single-use packaging after between 4 and 13 uses: about 4 to 6 uses to offset the extra greenhouse gases from manufacturing them, and 8 to 13 to offset the added energy involved in reuse. However, researchers said there is a catch — if even 5% of customers drive dedicated trips solely to return containers, the reuse system’s emissions can climb past those of single-use.

Reuse pays off when containers stay in circulation and returns fold into trips people already make. Upstream’s analysis puts break-even points across materials anywhere from 2 to 122 uses, with steel, glass, and ceramics good for thousands.

Where the Networks Operate Now

DeliverZero is in transition. The most established U.S. app launched in New York in 2019 and let diners add reusable containers to Uber Eats, DoorDash, Grubhub, and Toast orders for about $1, then return them to any participating restaurant. At its peak it reported a return rate near 98% across roughly 300 restaurants in New York, the Denver–Boulder area, Los Angeles, and the Bay Area. In 2025, food-locker company Minnow Technologies acquired DeliverZero and paused its operations to rebuild the platform; as of mid-2026 the companies describe a next-generation reuse system in development rather than a live consumer service. It is the one to watch for a relaunch.

Recirclable is up and running in the Northeast. This Boston-based program works like a library checkout: download the free app, order at a participating restaurant, and you are charged only if you fail to return the container within 14 day. The app has operated for about four years and expanded into Western Massachusetts in December 2025.

Reusables.com is for campuses and institutions. Its Tap-to-Reuse system tags cups and containers with RFID or QR codes and collects them through smart return bins. The company reports return rates near 99% in institutional dining, including 99.6% at Simon Fraser University, and ir raised a $3.6 million in funding. If you eat in a university or corporate cafeteria, this is the model you are most likely to encounter.

Vessel Works has operated since 2015, focusing on managing the flow of reusable cups in cafés in Boulder and Berkeley; it also licenses its system to other operators. On college campuses, Grubhub offers reuse through its partner Topanga. Because universities are where many young people establish lifelong habits, reuse on campuses is an encouraging growth market.

Some cities run their own systems: Ann Arbor, Michigan, for example, supplies the containers and lets restaurants opt in, giving residents a single program to return to.

Availability is still local and shifting, so the reliable move is to check what exists where you live before you order.

What You Can Do

Look for a reuse option at order time. In Uber Eats or DoorDash, check whether a reusable-container choice appears for restaurants near you; in a served metro, look for a local program like Recirclable; on campus, ask dining services what they run.

In Uber Eats or DoorDash, check whether a reusable-container choice appears for restaurants near you; in a served metro, look for a local program like Recirclable; on campus, ask dining services what they run. Bring your own container. Many restaurants will fill a clean container you bring for pickup orders — just ask. Carry reusable utensils so you can wave off the plastic set.

Many restaurants will fill a clean container you bring for pickup orders — just ask. Carry reusable utensils so you can wave off the plastic set. Ask for less. When reuse and BYO are not options, request no bag, skip the utensils, straws, and condiment packets you will not use (usually a single tap in delivery apps), and choose paper over foam.

When reuse and BYO are not options, request no bag, skip the utensils, straws, and condiment packets you will not use (usually a single tap in delivery apps), and choose paper over foam. Tell restaurants it matters. The reuse companies consistently find that diners will switch restaurants for a reuse option, and operators respond to that demand.

Handle what you do get wisely. Use the Earth911 Recycling Search to confirm what your area accepts, and keep “compostable” or bioplastic containers out of the recycling bin — most require industrial facilities and can contaminate a whole load.

Related Reading

Editor’s Note: This article was originally published on July 20, 2022, under the headline “Apps Rate Restaurants for Earth-Friendly Packaging,” was was substantially updated in July 2026.
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the growing adoption of reusable-container networks to reduce massive takeout packaging waste in the U.S., highlighting measurable environmental benefits and scalable programs in multiple cities and institutions. It provides detailed context on the environmental impact, operational models, and practical guidance for consumers, demonstrating significant positive real-world impact on waste reduction and sustainability.
articles.personality_title: Reusable containers cut takeout waste in U.S. cities and campuses
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Each year, U.S. restaurants throw away nearly 9 million tons of single-use takeout containers, cups, lids, and utensils. Most of this waste comes from delivery and takeout orders, creating a huge environmental problem.

**What happened** – New programs have started using reusable containers that customers borrow when ordering food and return later. These containers, made of durable materials like BPA-free plastic, can be used hundreds of times. Several networks, such as Recirclable in Boston and Reusables.com on college campuses, have launched systems where containers are cleaned and reused instead of thrown away. Some apps and services allow customers to easily order with reusable containers and return them at many participating restaurants.

**Impact** – Studies show reusable containers become better for the environment than single-use ones after just a few uses, cutting down on waste and pollution. For example, a University of Michigan study found that just 4 to 13 uses are needed to offset the extra energy used to make reusable containers. Return rates in some programs reach nearly 99%, meaning very little waste is created. These networks help reduce the huge pile of takeout trash and encourage more sustainable habits.

**What's next step** – Some programs are expanding to new cities and campuses. DeliverZero, a major player, is rebuilding its system for a 2026 relaunch. More restaurants and customers are expected to join reuse networks. People can also help by bringing their own containers or choosing reuse options when ordering takeout.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Borrowing and returning reusable containers for takeout is a growing, effective way to reduce millions of tons of single-use packaging waste across U.S. cities and campuses.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des contenants réutilisables réduisent les déchets des plats à emporter aux États-Unis
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Chaque année, les restaurants américains jettent près de 9 millions de tonnes de contenants jetables pour plats à emporter, y compris gobelets, couvercles et ustensiles. La majorité de ces déchets provient des commandes à emporter et en livraison.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – De nouveaux programmes proposent des contenants réutilisables que les clients empruntent lorsqu'ils commandent à emporter, puis rapportent. Ces contenants, fabriqués en plastique durable sans BPA, peuvent être utilisés des centaines de fois. Des réseaux comme Recirclable à Boston et Reusables.com sur les campus universitaires ont mis en place des systèmes où les contenants sont lavés et remis en circulation au lieu d’être jetés. Certaines applications permettent de commander facilement avec des contenants réutilisables et de les rapporter dans plusieurs restaurants partenaires.

**Impact** – Des études montrent que les contenants réutilisables deviennent meilleurs pour l’environnement après seulement quelques utilisations, réduisant ainsi les déchets et la pollution. Par exemple, une étude de l’Université du Michigan indique qu’il suffit de 4 à 13 utilisations pour compenser l’énergie supplémentaire nécessaire à leur fabrication. Le taux de retour atteint près de 99 % dans certains programmes, ce qui limite fortement les déchets. Ces réseaux contribuent à diminuer la montagne de déchets des plats à emporter et encouragent des habitudes plus durables.

**Prochaine étape** – Certains programmes s’étendent à de nouvelles villes et campus. DeliverZero, un acteur majeur, reconstruit son système pour un relancement en 2026. De plus en plus de restaurants et clients devraient rejoindre ces réseaux. Les consommateurs peuvent aussi apporter leurs propres contenants ou choisir les options réutilisables lors de leurs commandes.

**En une phrase** – Emprunter et rapporter des contenants réutilisables pour les plats à emporter est une solution efficace et en plein essor pour réduire des millions de tonnes de déchets jetables dans les villes et campus américains.
articles.personality_title_es: Contenedores reutilizables reducen residuos de comida para llevar en EE.UU.
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Cada año, los restaurantes en Estados Unidos desechan cerca de 9 millones de toneladas de envases, vasos, tapas y utensilios desechables para comida para llevar. La mayoría de estos residuos proviene de pedidos para llevar y entregas a domicilio.

**Qué pasó** – Nuevos programas ofrecen envases reutilizables que los clientes pueden pedir con su comida y luego devolver. Estos envases, hechos de plástico duradero sin BPA, pueden usarse cientos de veces. Redes como Recirclable en Boston y Reusables.com en campus universitarios han creado sistemas donde los envases se lavan y vuelven a usarse en lugar de desecharse. Algunas aplicaciones permiten pedir fácilmente con envases reutilizables y devolverlos en varios restaurantes participantes.

**Impacto** – Estudios demuestran que los envases reutilizables son mejores para el ambiente después de solo unos pocos usos, reduciendo residuos y contaminación. Por ejemplo, un estudio de la Universidad de Michigan encontró que se necesitan de 4 a 13 usos para compensar la energía extra usada en su fabricación. Algunas redes alcanzan tasas de devolución cercanas al 99 %, lo que genera muy pocos desechos. Estas redes ayudan a disminuir la gran cantidad de basura de comida para llevar y fomentan hábitos más sostenibles.

**Próximo paso** – Algunos programas están expandiéndose a nuevas ciudades y campus. DeliverZero, un actor importante, está reconstruyendo su sistema para relanzarlo en 2026. Se espera que más restaurantes y clientes se unan a estas redes. Las personas también pueden ayudar llevando sus propios envases o eligiendo opciones reutilizables al ordenar.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Pedir y devolver envases reutilizables para comida para llevar es una forma creciente y efectiva de reducir millones de toneladas de residuos desechables en ciudades y campus de EE.UU.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a cozy café scene featuring a stack of colorful, sturdy reusable takeout containers with subtle wear marks, surrounded by gentle hands exchanging them in a smooth, circular motion symbolizing borrowing and returning, set against a soft, natural palette of earthy tones and muted pastels.

articles.title: From lithium to nickel: How the UN is helping countries make the most of critical minerals
articles.summary: The world's shift to clean energy is driving huge demand for critical minerals such as lithium, nickel, cobalt and copper.
articles.content:
Digging them out of the ground may be the most straightforward part of the process.

Around the world, the United Nations is helping countries make sure these valuable resources create decent jobs, protect the environment and benefit local communities – not just global supply chains.

Argentina: Putting communities at the centre of the lithium boom

Argentina sits on some of the world's largest lithium reserves, making it a key supplier for the batteries used in electric vehicles and renewable energy.

The United Nations is helping ensure local people have a say in how that development unfolds. It supports dialogue between governments, companies, workers and Indigenous communities so they can tackle issues ranging from environmental protection to economic opportunities together.

The UN is also helping provinces strengthen their financial planning so more of the benefits from lithium stay in local communities, while supporting skills training and efforts to bring more women into the sector.

Democratic Republic of the Congo: Turning mineral wealth into economic growth

The Democratic Republic of the Congo produces around 70 per cent of the world's cobalt, a mineral that is essential for many rechargeable batteries used in electric vehicles and energy storage.

© Adobe Stock/Erberto Zani

The United Nations is supporting efforts to ensure that the country's critical mineral wealth contributes to broader economic development. This includes helping strengthen public policy, promoting responsible business conduct and supporting local businesses so they can participate in emerging battery and electric vehicle supply chains.

Indonesia: Getting more value from nickel

Indonesia is a major producer of nickel, a mineral that is essential for many electric vehicle batteries.

Rather than relying mainly on exports of raw materials, the country is working to expand higher-value manufacturing at home.

© Adobe Stock/Mirwanto

The UN is supporting that effort by helping improve industrial production standards, promote cleaner manufacturing and increase domestic value addition across critical-mineral supply chains.

The UN is also helping strengthen occupational safety and health in mining, develop the green skills needed for a changing economy and share Indonesia's experience with other mineral-producing countries through South-South cooperation on sustainable mining and responsible resource governance.

Kazakhstan: Making mining cleaner and more accountable

Kazakhstan is working to strengthen the governance of its critical mineral sector as global demand for these resources continues to grow.

The United Nations is supporting efforts to strengthen oversight of critical mineral supply chains by helping align national frameworks with international standards and identify risks linked to crime, corruption and weak accountability.

Support also includes improving mine safety and labour standards, promoting responsible business conduct in line with the UN Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights, and exploring ways to recover valuable raw materials from electronic waste to reduce the need for new extraction.

Zambia: Mining new local jobs

Zambia's rich mineral resources have an important role to play in the clean energy transition. The United Nations is helping ensure that mining also creates new opportunities for workers, local businesses and communities.

Support includes developing a national mining skills strategy, preparing workers for jobs in mining and battery value chains, and helping small and medium-sized businesses participate in emerging electric vehicle supply chains.

The UN is also working with artisanal and small-scale miners to improve safety, strengthen environmental safeguards and support formalization.

Local communities are taking part in monitoring mining's environmental impacts alongside government, universities and mining companies, helping build trust around mining projects.

Material gathered by the UN Development Coordination Office
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article describes the United Nations' efforts to ensure that the extraction and use of critical minerals for clean energy benefit local communities, improve environmental protection, create decent jobs, and promote responsible mining practices across multiple countries. This has broad significance for the global clean energy transition and sustainable development, with detailed examples and concrete actions supporting positive real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: How the UN helps countries use critical minerals for clean energy and local benefits
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The world is using more clean energy, which needs minerals like lithium, nickel, cobalt, and copper. These minerals come from mining, but mining can harm communities and the environment if not done carefully.

**What happened** – The United Nations is working with countries that have these important minerals to make sure mining creates good jobs, protects nature, and helps local people. In Argentina, the UN supports talks between governments, companies, and Indigenous groups about lithium mining. In the Democratic Republic of the Congo, it promotes fair business rules and helps local businesses grow with cobalt mining. In Indonesia, the UN helps improve nickel processing to add more value inside the country. In Kazakhstan, it works to make mining safer and cleaner, while fighting corruption. In Zambia, the UN supports training workers and small businesses and helps local communities watch over mining's effects.

**Impact** – This work is important because it connects mining to real benefits for people who live near the mines, not just big companies. It helps countries keep more money from their minerals and protects the environment. The UN’s approach also encourages safer and fairer mining, which can inspire other countries to do the same.

**What's next step** – These countries will keep improving how they manage mining with the UN’s help. They plan to train more workers, support businesses, and share their experiences with other nations. This can lead to safer mining, better jobs, and cleaner production in more places.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The United Nations is helping mineral-rich countries turn mining into safer, fairer, and more valuable work that benefits local communities and supports clean energy.
articles.personality_title_fr: Comment l’ONU aide les pays à utiliser les minéraux essentiels pour l’énergie propre et le développement local
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le monde utilise de plus en plus d’énergie propre, qui nécessite des minéraux comme le lithium, le nickel, le cobalt et le cuivre. Leur extraction peut être nuisible aux communautés et à l’environnement si elle n’est pas bien encadrée.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – L’Organisation des Nations Unies travaille avec les pays qui possèdent ces minéraux importants pour que l’exploitation minière crée de bons emplois, protège la nature et profite aux habitants locaux. En Argentine, l’ONU soutient le dialogue entre gouvernements, entreprises et communautés autochtones sur l’extraction du lithium. En République démocratique du Congo, elle encourage des règles commerciales équitables et aide les entreprises locales grâce à l’exploitation du cobalt. En Indonésie, l’ONU améliore la transformation du nickel pour augmenter sa valeur dans le pays. Au Kazakhstan, elle rend l’exploitation minière plus sûre et plus propre, tout en combattant la corruption. En Zambie, l’ONU forme les travailleurs, soutient les petites entreprises et aide les communautés à surveiller l’impact de l’exploitation minière.

**Impact** – Ce travail est important car il permet aux populations vivant près des mines de bénéficier directement de l’exploitation, et pas seulement aux grandes entreprises. Il aide les pays à garder plus d’argent de leurs minéraux et à protéger l’environnement. L’approche de l’ONU encourage aussi une exploitation plus sûre et plus juste, ce qui peut inspirer d’autres pays.

**Prochaine étape** – Ces pays vont continuer à améliorer la gestion minière avec le soutien de l’ONU. Ils prévoient de former davantage de travailleurs, d’aider les entreprises et de partager leurs expériences avec d’autres nations. Cela peut conduire à une exploitation minière plus sûre, de meilleurs emplois et une production plus propre ailleurs.

**En une phrase** – L’ONU aide les pays riches en minéraux à transformer l’exploitation minière en un travail plus sûr, plus juste et plus rentable, au bénéfice des communautés locales et de l’énergie propre.
articles.personality_title_es: Cómo la ONU ayuda a los países a usar minerales clave para la energía limpia y el beneficio local
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El mundo está usando más energía limpia, que necesita minerales como litio, níquel, cobalto y cobre. Extraer estos minerales puede dañar a las comunidades y al medio ambiente si no se hace con cuidado.

**Qué pasó** – Las Naciones Unidas están trabajando con países que tienen estos minerales importantes para que la minería cree buenos empleos, proteja la naturaleza y ayude a las personas locales. En Argentina, la ONU apoya el diálogo entre gobiernos, empresas y comunidades indígenas sobre la minería del litio. En la República Democrática del Congo, promueve reglas comerciales justas y ayuda a las empresas locales con la minería del cobalto. En Indonesia, la ONU ayuda a mejorar el procesamiento del níquel para agregar más valor dentro del país. En Kazajistán, trabaja para hacer la minería más segura y limpia, y para combatir la corrupción. En Zambia, la ONU apoya la formación de trabajadores y pequeñas empresas y ayuda a las comunidades a supervisar los efectos de la minería.

**Impacto** – Este trabajo es importante porque conecta la minería con beneficios reales para las personas que viven cerca de las minas, no solo para las grandes empresas. Ayuda a los países a conservar más dinero de sus minerales y a proteger el medio ambiente. El enfoque de la ONU también fomenta una minería más segura y justa, que puede inspirar a otros países.

**Próximo paso** – Estos países seguirán mejorando la forma en que gestionan la minería con la ayuda de la ONU. Planean formar a más trabajadores, apoyar a las empresas y compartir sus experiencias con otras naciones. Esto puede llevar a una minería más segura, mejores empleos y una producción más limpia en más lugares.

**Una frase clave** – La ONU está ayudando a los países ricos en minerales a convertir la minería en un trabajo más seguro, justo y valioso que beneficia a las comunidades locales y apoya la energía limpia.
articles.image_prompt: A harmonious landscape featuring stylized mineral crystals of lithium, cobalt, nickel, and other critical elements emerging from the earth, interconnected by flowing golden threads symbolizing collaboration and shared prosperity, with abstract silhouettes of diverse hands gently supporting the crystals, set against a warm, softly colored backdrop representing community, environmental care, and sustainable growth.
